coredata.dc:title: A computational investigation of 11B and 15N chemical shielding tensors as well as local aromaticity based on NICS characterization in the N/B doped triangular graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Anafcheh, Maryam
Ghafouri, Reza
coredata.dc:description: We have performed density functional theory calculations on a representative set of N/B doped triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to investigate the effect of N/B doping on the chemical shielding (CS) parameters of nitrogen and boron nuclei, and local aromaticity based on NICS characterization. The results show that CS parameters are so sensitive to the doping position. The hexagonal lattice of the triangular GQDs consists of two sets of sublattices, in each of which the CS parameters of N/B sites appear in two layers (for sites at the edges or in the inner area of sublattice). Our results also show distinct aromaticity distribution inside these molecules such that NICS(1) at the ring centers of six hexagons has maximum values (from −8.9 to −10.2ppm), which can be explained within the framework of the Clar’s Sextet Theory. When N dopant is located in the inner area, NICS(1) index shows antiaromaticity (9.1–12.5) for the three fused hexagons around the nitrogen dopant while the substituted N dopants at the edges of GQDs diminish aromaticity in the pyridine and aza naphthalene like rings. In all the B-doped GQDs, NICS shows antiaromaticity for hexagons with B dopants (9.0–12.0ppm) and nonaromaticity for the other hexagons.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triangular graphene quantum dots
NMR
NICS
DFT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic structure of a zigzag-edged triangular GQD with edge size n =6, where the letters denote the different doping positions; A and B represent the two sublattices in the hexagonal lattice of graphene.
Diagrams of calculated 15N CSI and CSA values for N-doped (a) and B-doped (b) GQDs with different configurations.
The calculated 15N and 11B NMR parameters versus C–N and C–B bond lengths (Å) in the N-doped and B-doped GQDs.
NICS values at the ring centers and points 1Å above (in the parentheses) for the pure GQD and N-A0, N-A4, N-B4, B-A2 and B-B5 models.
C–N and C–B bond lengths (Å) along with isotropic and anisotropic chemical shieldings (CSI and CSA) of the corresponding N and B sites (ppm) in the N-doped and B-doped GQDs.

head.ce:title: A computational investigation of 11B and 15N chemical shielding tensors as well as local aromaticity based on NICS characterization in the N/B doped triangular graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahr-e-Ray Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Local aromaticity based on NICS characterization in the N/B doped triangular graphene quantum dots.

We have performed density functional theory calculations on a representative set of N/B doped triangular graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to investigate the effect of N/B doping on the chemical shielding (CS) parameters of nitrogen and boron nuclei, and local aromaticity based on NICS characterization. The results show that CS parameters are so sensitive to the doping position. The hexagonal lattice of the triangular GQDs consists of two sets of sublattices, in each of which the CS parameters of N/B sites appear in two layers (for sites at the edges or in the inner area of sublattice). Our results also show distinct aromaticity distribution inside these molecules such that NICS(1) at the ring centers of six hexagons has maximum values (from −8.9 to −10.2ppm), which can be explained within the framework of the Clar’s Sextet Theory. When N dopant is located in the inner area, NICS(1) index shows antiaromaticity (9.1–12.5) for the three fused hexagons around the nitrogen dopant while the substituted N dopants at the edges of GQDs diminish aromaticity in the pyridine and aza naphthalene like rings. In all the B-doped GQDs, NICS shows antiaromaticity for hexagons with B dopants (9.0–12.0ppm) and nonaromaticity for the other hexagons.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanostructures have stimulated a lot of interest in condensed matter physics and material sciences due to their unique mechanical, optical, and electronic properties [1–3]. Among these nanostructures, graphene became one exciting subject in the recent research [4–7]. Many of its interesting physical properties and potential technological applications, including room-temperature quantum Hall effect, near ballistic transport at room temperature, ultrahigh massless carrier mobilities, and tunable energy gaps, make it different from ordinary compounds and a promising material in nanoelectronics and nanodevices [8,9]. The perfect graphene sheet is a semimetal with a zero band gap, and its size and edges have significant effects on its electronic structure [5]. On the other hand, it is suggested that some graphene nanostructures, such as one-dimensional graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), be semiconductors with direct energy band gaps [8,10], which have allowed the possibility of exploring low dimensional transport and perspective for carbon-based nanoelectronics [11–13]. These unique properties make GQD a special candidate for building block in nanodevices including solid-state qubits, transistors, spin memory, and optoelectronics applications [14–16]. Recently, several theoretical studies have been performed to investigate the electronic properties and magnetism of different shaped zero-dimensional GQDs [17,18]. Meanwhile, the energy gaps for GQDs are predicted to be governed by introducing other elements as dopants into their structures, and also by their sizes [10,19]. For example Yu et al. [10] investigated the electronic properties of N/B doped triangular GQDs with hydrogen passivated zigzag edges using DFT calculations, showing that adjusting N/B doping sites is one way to control the electronic properties of GQDs.

To date, most studies on graphene nanostructures have been focused on its mesoscopic properties related to electronic properties. However, it is necessary to develop standard, easily applicable methods in order to understand the influence of size, defects, impurities and dopants on the aforementioned properties, and hence to qualify the type, and quantify the influences induced to a given sample. One of the most versatile experimental tools to study the local geometries and electronic structures of molecules and solids is nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), such that over the past few years an increasing amount of experimental and theoretical NMR data for nanostructures such as nanotubes and fullerenes became available [20–22]. Due to high sensitivity to the electronic structure in their environment, magnetically active nuclei can be used to probe the nearby shielding influences. For example, this is realized that the chemical shift of an atom in the inner and outer surfaces covered by a sea of π electrons should be remarkable influenced due to the ring currents (cyclic delocalization of electrons). Several criteria have been introduced to rationalize and quantify this property [23,24]. The most important ones that are based on NMR chemical shifts and diamagnetic susceptibilities [23] include the nucleus-independent chemical shifts (NICS) which was introduced as a simple and efficient aromaticity probe by Schleyer et al. [25] in 1996. To match the familiar NMR convention, NICS indices correspond to the negative of magnetic shielding computed at chosen points in the vicinity of molecules. NICS is typically computed at ring centers, at points above, and even as grids in and around the molecule. Significantly negative NICS values (i.e., magnetically shielded) in interior positions of rings or cages indicate the presence of induced diatropic ring currents or “aromaticity”, whereas positive values (i.e., deshielded) denote paratropic ring currents and “antiaromaticity”. Being directly based on cyclic electron delocalization, the essence of aromaticity, NICS has several advantages over many other aromaticity criteria: NICS does not require reference standards, NICS correlates well with other aromaticity indexes based on energetic, geometric, and other magnetic criteria [26,27], and finally NICS can be computed easily using standard quantum chemical programs. According to direct experimental validations, the NICS is now well accepted by the chemical community. In addition, the grid distribution of NICS around molecule has been widely employed [28,29] to provide better insights of the overall molecular magnetic properties.

A GQD can be considered as a special case of the more general, arbitrarily shaped graphene fragments that are finite in both dimensions which are referred as graphene nanoflakes (GNFs). All these structures have zero intrinsic spin except the zigzag-edged triangular GQD in which a linear-scaling net spin arises due to topological frustration of the π-bonds. From a chemical point of view, these hydrogen-terminated graphene triangles can be called colossal hydrocarbon radicals except that the former are not as reactive because in a conjugated π-system the delocalization of the unpaired spin orbital substantially lowers its energy [14]. Therefore, in this work, we aim to investigate the magnetic properties of N/B doped triangular GQDs with hydrogen passivated zigzag edges by computing the grid distribution of NICS values around molecules in order to monitoring shielding effects and the behavior of magnetic field around these specific nanostructures. These results are compared to those of parent GQD to establish whether it depends on their doping site and/or their symmetry/topology. We also study 11B and 15N chemical shielding (CS) tensors of N/B doped triangular GQDs and try to explore how N/B doping influences the GQDs. CS tensors which are reproduced reliably by performing high-level quantum chemical calculations provide highly valuable information about electrostatic environment around the nucleus, which feels subtle changes by any perturbations [30]. The calculations of NMR parameters using ab initio techniques have found the ability of quickly evaluating and correlating the magnitude of CS tensors with variations in bond angles, bond lengths, and the nearest neighboring interactions, which has increased the significance of utilizing these parameters in exploring molecular structures [31,32].

According to the Yu et al. [10] work, we select 14 different doping positions in the zigzag triangular GQD, as shown in Fig. 1. The doping positions A0∼A5 and A m lie in the sublattice A, while B0∼B5 and B n are located in the sublattice B. In our work, the considered configurations for N-doped GQDs are represented by N-A0∼N-A5, N-A m , N-B0∼N-B5, and N-B n , while for B-doped GQDs, those cases are labeled by B-A0∼B-A5, B-A m , B-B0∼B-B5, and B-B n . Geometries of all systems are allowed to fully relax during the B3LYP/6-31G* optimization process [34]. The standard 6-31G* basis set is employed for being affordable and accurate enough for geometry optimization of even large molecules [35,36]. Then, the optimized structures of N/B doped triangular GQDs and their parent are subjected to the calculation of the related properties. 11B and 15N CS tensors are calculated at the B3LYP/6-31G* level based on the gauge independent atomic orbital (GIAO) approach [37]. Dokalik et al. [38], in their study on the validity of different computational methods for calculating N chemical shieldings in a series of nitrogen-containing heterocycles (5-, 6-, 5+6-, 6+6- and 6+6+6-membered rings), verified the accuracy and adequacy of this method for producing reliable results in comparison with the available experimental values. The calculated CS tensors in the principal axes system (PAS) (σ 33 > σ 22 > σ 11) are converted to the measurable NMR parameters: chemical shielding isotropy (CSI) and chemical shielding anisotropy (CSA), using the following equations [39]:
(1)
CSI
( ppm ) = 1 / 3 (
σ
11
+
σ
22
+
σ
33
)
(2)
CSA
( ppm ) =
σ
33
- (
σ
11
+
σ
22
) / 2
To calculate NICS indices, ghost atoms are placed at the ring centers and also at the points 1Å above.

Triangular GQD C61H21 is chosen with hydrogen passivated zigzag edges, which is fully optimized at the UB3LYP/6-31G* level, as the parent molecule for doping. The structural properties of triangular N/B-doped GQDs such as C–B bond lengths are found to be 1.495–1.548 and 1.481–1.523Å when B dopant is located at the zigzag edges and in the inner area, respectively, while C–N bond lengths are contracted, ranging from 1.378 to 1.395Å at the zigzag edges and from 1.402 to 1.414Å in the inner area, consistent with the theoretical values reported by Yu et al. [10].

Fourteen doping positions are selected in the zigzag triangular GQD with size n =6, as shown in Fig. 1. The doping positions A0∼A5 and A m lie in the sublattice A, while B0∼B5 and B n are located in the sublattice B. The corresponding isotropic and anisotropic chemical shieldings (CSI and CSA) of N sites for these N-doped GQDs are given in Table 1 , and also plotted in Fig. 2 . Differences in the values of CSI parameters clarify diversity in the electron densities of nitrogen nuclei, and it is expected that the doping position plays an important role in the electronic structures. Therefore, due to the high sensitivity of CS tensors to the electronic densities at the sites of magnetic nuclei, an attempt is made to correlate 15N chemical shielding parameters with the doping position of N nuclei in GQDs. Based on our results, there are four distinct ranges of 15N chemical shielding parameters which are related to each of the nonequivalent magnetic sites of N atoms: the CSI of nitrogen sites which are located at sublattice A appear in two layers with the values of 122.22–127.08 and 139.31–145.90ppm such that the former range is related to the N dopants located in the inner area of sublattice A (N-A0, N-A1, N-A2, and N-A m ) and the latter one is related to the N dopants positioned at the zigzag edges of subalttice A (N-A3, N-A4, and N-A5). The N doping of sublattice B causes remarkable decreased 15N CSI values. The isotropic chemical shieldings of N sites at sublattice B also appear in two layers: the CSI values of nitrogen dopants which lie at the edges of sublattice B appear in the range of 63.04–87.03ppm while the CSI values of nitrogen dopants in the inner area of sublattice B increase to 89.03–92.21ppm, which could be due to the changes in the electrostatic environment originating from the positions of the corresponding sites on the surface of the GQDs. Parallel to the CSI, the calculated CSA values of N sites in each sublattice are divided into two similar groups each of which belongs to N sites located at the zigzag edges or in the inner area of the surface of GQDs with similar chemical environments (Fig. 2). Since Yu et al. [10] showed similar pattern for the relative energies of the N-doped GQDs, it would be interesting to make an attempt to connect the structural parameters within the molecules to the 15N NMR parameters, which should be helpful in identifying structural patterns from the experimental NMR spectrum.

To this aim, Fig. 3 depicts the calculated 15N chemical shielding isotropy and anisotropy (CSI and CSA) parameters versus C–N bond lengths for the N-doped GQDs. A decreasing trend is observed for the CSI values of N sites which are located at sublattice A as the C–N bond lengths in these N-doped GQDs increase while 15N CSA shows an upward trend. Reverse trend is observed for N sites in sublattice B (Fig. 3), i.e., the CSI of N sites gradually increases from 63.04ppm in the N-B5 model with the C–N bond length of 1.379Å to 92.21ppm in the N-B1 model with the C–N bond length of 1.410Å. This discrepancy between the chemical shielding parameters of N sites at sublattices A and B could be mainly dependent on the orientations of the principal components of 15N chemical shielding tensors. According to the mentioned Eqs. (1) and (2), CSI parameter refers to the averaged electronic density at the atomic sites while CSA parameter indicates the difference between the distribution of the electronic densities perpendicular to the molecular plane (z axis) and within the molecular plane (x–y axes). The larger magnitudes of CSI and the smaller values of CSA for nitrogen atoms at sublattice A indicate that the distribution of electronic density at N sites increases in the molecular plane while the smaller magnitudes of CSI and the larger values of CSA for nitrogen atoms at sublattice B indicate that the distribution of electronic density around nitrogen atoms are more oriented perpendicular to the molecular plane.

According to Fig. 2, the CSI and CSA parameters of boron nuclei in the B-doped GQDs are also divided into layers. However, the slight differences between 11B CSI values clarify diminutive diversity in the electron densities of boron nuclei. Furthermore, CSI values depend on the doping position; in contrast to the N-doped GQDs, the GQDs with B doped in the inner region of sublattice A have a relatively larger CSI values in comparison to the case with B doped at zigzag edges, while a reverse trend is observed for the B models of GQDs, see Table 1. For example in the GQDs with B doped in sublattice A, the CSI values of boron nuclei (Fig. 2) are divided into two layers with the values of 84.07–88.07ppm and 71.76–76.26ppm related to the B dopants at the zigzag edges and in the inner region of the GQD surface, respectively. Again in contrast to N-doped GQDs, 11B CSI values for GQD with B doped at sublattice B are larger than those of B doped at sublattice A. Further, a decreasing trend is observed for the CSI values of B sites at sublattice A as C–B bond lengths increases while reverse trend is observed for B sites at sublattice B (see Fig. 2), i.e., 11B CSI gradually decreases from 99.74ppm in the B-B4 model with the C–B bond length of 1.500Å to 91.03ppm in the B-B0 model with the C–B bond length of 1.482Å. On the other hand, for both sublattices A and B, CSA of B sites show an upward trend as C–B bond length increases.

To evaluate the local effects of magnetic field in the N/B-doped GQDs as well as the mobility of electrons on the GQD surfaces, we calculate NICS(0) (at ring centers) and NICS(+1) (1Å above ring centers) to compare the degree of aromaticity in the different rings of the GQDs. Delocalization in GQDs is not expected to be as ideal as in benzene, similar to polycyclic aromatic compounds, because of the fusion of two or more aromatic rings that perturbs the delocalization of electrons. Moreover, it is shown that uniform magnetic fields above molecular planes, which are observed in the graphite-like structures, do not appear. This leads to the question about the degree of aromaticity in GQDs. The application of local criteria such as NICS [23,24] allow researchers to consider this variation by taking into account the local aromaticity, i.e., by calculating the NICS indexes for the individual rings.

Although the NICS values at the geometric center of a ring have been proven to be an efficient way to quantify aromaticity, NICS(1), where the π orbitals have their maximum density, has been recommended as being a better measure of the π electron delocalization in comparison to NICS(0), in order to avoid the complicating influence of σ orbital contributions [23]. With this initial thought in mind, the calculated NICS(0) and NICS(1) values are presented in Fig. 4 which shows a significant variation, especially in NICS(1), for different rings, depending dramatically on the topological positions of the rings. It is worth noting that inside the pure GQD molecule NICS(1) has maximum values, Fig. 4, in six hexagons (−8.9 to −10.2ppm) whereas shows low aromaticity for the other hexagons, ranging from −4.2 to −6.0ppm. These values may be compared with NICS(1)=−10.2ppm for benzene itself [23]. Similar trend is observed for NICS(0) at the ring centers of hexagons. The appearance of such distinct aromaticities is explained within the framework of the Clar’s Sextet Theory [40,41]. According to the Clar characteristics, the rings which have the highest aromatic characters are those that belong to the class of “fully benzenoid” rings, i.e., the ones containing plentiful π-electrons relative to other rings. Based on the Clar postulate, individual benzenoid rings in polycyclic compounds such as GQDs can be regarded as local benzene-like regions [42].

In order to investigate the system-size effect on the calculated NMR parameters, zigzag-edged triangular GQDs with different sizes are considered. A GQD can be constructed with nonzero spin by stitching together hexagonal units that by themselves have zero spin. Starting with a zigzag-edged triangular GQD with 6-edge carbon atoms and adding one and two additional layers of hexagonal units to it, it produces a zigzag-edged triangular GNF with 7 and 8 edge carbon atoms. Then we perform the same calculations on pristine zigzag-edged triangular GQDs with edge size n =7 and 8. Comparing the obtained results for GQDs with 6, 7 and 8-edge carbon atoms, we emphasize that the GQDs fully supporting framework of the Clar’s Sextet Theory and composed of individual benzenoid rings with the NICS values ranging from −9 to −10.5 which surrounded with low aromatic rings with the NICS values ranging from −4.2 to −6.0ppm. The plots of NICS(0) and NICS(1) for the pure GQDs with 6, 7 and 8-edge carbon atoms are shown in Figs. S1 and S2 of the Supplementary material.

The application of NICS makes it possible to analyze the problem of changes of aromaticity due to the replacement of the carbon atom by boron/nitrogen atoms, both in a local and global sense. Based on our results, there are three local structures around the nitrogen nuclei in all the N-doped GQDs, illustrated in Fig. 4. When N dopant is located in the inner area, between three fused hexagons, a Cycl[3,3,3]azine is formed, and when N dopant is located at the zigzag edges of sublattice A or B a pyridine or an aza naphthalene like substructure appear, respectively. NICS(1) index shows antiaromaticity for the three fused hexagons in Cycl[3,3,3]azine like substructure: 9.1–12.5ppm which is in agreement with those reported previously for Cycl[3,3,3]azine derivatives [43]. Indeed, the substituted N atom diminishes aromaticity in pyridine and aza naphthalene like rings. Thus, compensation between diatropic and paratropic ring currents leads to negligible NICS values ranging from −0.7 to +0.7ppm, in contrast to the reported NICS for individual pyridine and aza naphthalene which show more and less aromaticity than benzene, respectively [23]. NICS shows low aromaticity for the other hexagons of the N-doped GQDs with NICS values ranging from −4.4 to −6.0ppm. On the other hand, in all the B-doped GQDs, the calculated NICS(1) values show antiaromaticity (9.0–12.0ppm) for the hexagons including B dopants, and nonaromaticity for the other hexagons. It is interestingly found that NICS(1) for the three fused hexagons in Cycl[3,3,3]azine like substructure in the N-doped GQD with 8-edge carbon atoms (N-A0) do not show any significant change in compare to those of smaller model. Similar behaviors are observed for B-doped GQD with 8-edge carbon atoms (B-A2). The plots of NICS (0) and NICS (1) for the pure GQDs with 6, 7 and 8-edge carbon atoms have been shown in Fig. S2 of the Supplementary material.

We have applied DFT calculations to investigate the effect of N/B doping on the electronic and magnetic properties of a selected triangular GQD with hydrogen passivated edges, with zigzag edge size n =6. From the evaluated CS parameters and NICS values at the ring centers (NICS(0)) and 1Å above (NICS(1)) for the N/B doped GQDs, the following results are worth pointing out. First, chemical shielding parameters are so sensitive to the doping position such that the CS parameters of N/B dopants at each sublattice (A and B) appear in two layers (related to the N/B dopants at the edges and in the inner area of sublattice), due to changes in the electrostatic environment originating from the positions of the corresponding sites on the surface of the GQDs. Second, in the pure GQD, NICS(1) has minimum value in six hexagons (from −8.9 to −10.2ppm) whereas it shows low aromaticity for the other hexagons. Such distinct aromaticitis can be explained within the framework of the Clar’s Sextet Theory. Third, when N dopant is located in the inner area, a Cycl[3,3,3]azine is formed, NICS shows antiaromaticity for the three fused hexagons with Cyclazine like substructure (9.1–12.5), while the substituted N atoms at the edges of GQDs diminish aromaticity in pyridine and aza naphthalene like rings. Fourth, in all the B-doped GQDs, hexagons with B nuclei show antiaromaticity (9.0–12.0ppm) while the other hexagons are nonaromatic.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.spmi.2013.07.018.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material contains Figs. S1 and S2.
